elements.sent-2411-001: We introduce and study the logarithmic $p$-Laplacian $L_{\Delta_p}$, which emerges from the formal derivative of the fractional $p$-Laplacian $(-\Delta_p)^s$ at $s=0$.
elements.sent-2411-002: This operator is nonlocal, has logarithmic order, and is the nonlinear version of the newly developed logarithmic Laplacian operator \cite{CW19 .}
elements.sent-2411-003: We present a variational framework to study the Dirichlet problems involving the $L_{\Delta_p}$ in bounded domains.
elements.sent-2411-004: This allows us to investigate the connection between the first Dirichlet eigenvalue and eigenfunction of the fractional $p$-Laplacian and the logarithmic $p$-Laplacian.
elements.sent-2411-005: As a consequence, we deduce a Faber-Krahn inequality for the first Dirichlet eigenvalue of $L_{\Delta_p}$.
elements.sent-2411-006: We discuss maximum and comparison principles for $L_{\Delta_p}$ in bounded domains and demonstrate that the validity of these depends on the sign of the first Dirichlet eigenvalue of $L_{\Delta_p}$.
elements.sent-2411-007: In addition, we prove that the first Dirichlet eigenfunction of $L_{\Delta_p}$ is bounded.
elements.sent-2411-008: Furthermore, we establish a boundary Hardy-type inequality for the spaces associated with the weak formulation of the logarithmic $p$-Laplacian.
elements.sent-2411-009: In recent decades there has been a growing interest in the understanding of boundary value problems involving nonlocal integro-differential operators --- the most prominent example is given by the fractional Laplace operator.
elements.sent-2411-010: Fueled by this interest, also nonlinear nonlocal interactions have been studied extensively \cite{L14,L16,IMS16,BP16,PQ16,MPSY16,CKP16,ILPS16,BLS18,IMP23 with a prominent example being given by the fractional $p$-Laplace operator.}
elements.sent-2411-011: This operator is given by \begin{equation}(-\Delta_p)^su(x) =C_{N,s,p}\,p.v.\int_{\R^N}\frac{|u(x)-u(y)|^{p-2}(u(x)-u(y))}{|x-y|^{N+sp}}\,dy,\end{equation} where $p\in(1,\infty)$, $s\in(0,1)$, p.v.\ stands for the Cauchy principal value, and $u$ is suitably regular at $x\in\R^N$ and integrable at infinity with respect to the kernel $z\mapsto |z|^{-N-sp}$.
elements.sent-2411-012: The constant $C_{N,s,p}$ here is chosen in the particular case $p=2$ such that $(-\Delta_2)^s$ has the Fourier symbol $|\cdot|^{2s}$.
elements.sent-2411-013: With this, $(-\Delta_2)^s$ can easily be seen as an intermediate operator between the identity operator and the Laplacian $-\Delta=-\sum_{k=1 ^{N}\partial_{kk}$.}
elements.sent-2411-014:
Similarly, for $p
eq 2$, the fractional $p$-Laplace operator can be seen as an intermediate operator between the identity and the $p$-Laplacian $-\Delta_p$ given by $-\Delta_pu=-\div(|
abla u|^{p-2}
abla u)$.
elements.sent-2411-015: However, in this general case an appropriate constant is not clear, see the discussions in \cite{TGV21,F23 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Here, we choose $C_{N,s,p}$ such that the limits \begin{equation}\lim_{s\to0^+}(-\Delta_p)^su(x)=|u(x)|^{p-2}u(x)\quadand\quad \lim_{s\to1^-}(-\Delta_p)^su(x)=-\Delta_pu(x)\end{equation} hold for smooth compactly supported functions, see Section \ref{constant .}
elements.sent-2411-017: The goal of this work is to find a suitable operator $L_{\Delta_p}$ to improve the understanding at the limit $s\to 0^+$.
elements.sent-2411-018: To be precise, we define an operator $L_{\Delta_p}$, which we call the logarithmic $p$-Laplace operator, such that the expansion \begin{equation}\label{simple expansion} (-\Delta_p)^su(x)=|u(x)|^{p-2}u(x)+sL_{\Delta_p}u(x)+o(s)\quad\text{for $s\to 0^+$}\end{equation} holds for a suitable class of functions $u$.
elements.sent-2411-019: In the linear case that is $p=2$, the study of the expansion \eqref{simple expansion has been started in \cite{CW19 , where the authors show that it holds \begin{equation}L_{\Delta_2}u(x)=C_N\int_{B_1(x)}\frac{u(x)-u(y)}{|x-y|^N}\,dy-C_N\int_{\R^N\setminus B_1(x)}\frac{u(y)}{|x-y|^{N}}\,dy+\rho_Nu(x),\end{equation} where $C_N=\frac{\Gamma(N/2) {\pi^{N/2 }$ and $\rho_N=2\ln 2-\gamma+\psi(N/2)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Here and below, $\psi=\Gamma'/\Gamma$ denotes the Digamma function, $\Gamma$ is the Gamma function, and $\gamma=-\Gamma'(1)$ is the Euler-Mascheroni constant.
elements.sent-2411-021: Moreover, it is shown in \cite{CW19 that $L_{\Delta_2}$ indeed has the Fourier symbol $2\ln|\cdot|$.}

elements.sent-2411-023: The investigation of fractional problems in this regime is especially relevant to optimization problems where the optimal order $s$ is small.
elements.sent-2411-024: Such small order limits are particularly important in applications like image processing and population dynamics, as discussed in references \cite{AnBa, PeVe, SpVa .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Having the expansion at zero, there have been several works on the study of the expansion of eigenvalues, eigenfunctions, and certain solutions, see \cite{JSW20,HS21,FJW22 .}

elements.sent-2411-027: The logarithmic Laplacian also arises in the geometric context of the 0-fractional perimeter, see \cite{LuNoPo .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Inspired by the aforementioned works, we aim to generalize these results to the nonlinear case.
elements.sent-2411-029: In particular, we give an explicit representation of the operator $L_{\Delta_p}$ in \eqref{simple expansion , which, unlike its linear counterpart, is both nonlinear and of logarithmic order.}

elements.subsection-2411-006: On the normalization constant of the fractional \texorpdfstring{$p$}
elements.sent-2411-111: Recall the definition of the fractional $p$-Laplacian $(-\Delta_p)^su$ for $u$ sufficiently regular in the introduction, where the normalization constant $C_{N,s,p}$ is given by \begin{equation}C_{N,s,p}=\left\{ \frac{sp\,2^{2s-2}\,\Gamma\left(\frac{N+sp}{2}\right)}{\pi^{\frac{N-1}{2}}\Gamma(1-s)\Gamma\left(\frac{p+1}{2}\right)}  if  s>\frac{1}{2}, \frac{sp\,2^{2s-1}\,\Gamma\left(\frac{N+sp}{2}\right)}{\pi^{\frac{N}{2}}\Gamma(1-s) }  if s\leq \frac{1}{2}. \right.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-112: The following is well-known, however, we include its proof for the reader's convenience and completeness.
elements.sent-2411-115: Then $(-\Delta_p)^su(x)$ is well-defined for any $x\in \R^N$ and it holds \begin{equation}\lim_{s\to0^+}(-\Delta_p)^su(x)=g(u(x))\quadfor all $x\in \R^N$.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-118: Moreover, after fixing $x\in \R^N$, we have, with $R>0$ such that $\overline{\supp\,u}\subset B_R(x)$, \begin{equation}(-\Delta_p)^su(x) =C_{N,s,p}\int_{B_R(x)}\frac{g(u(x)-u(y))}{|x-y|^{N+sp}}\,dy+ C_{N,s,p}\int_{B_R^c(x)}\frac{g(u(x)-u(y))}{|x-y|^{N+sp}}\,dy =C_{N,s,p}\int_{B_R(x)}\frac{g(u(x)-u(y))}{|x-y|^{N+sp}}\,dy+ g(u(x))C_{N,s,p}\int_{B_R^c(x)}|x-y|^{-N-sp}\,dy,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\Bigg|\int_{B_R(x)}\frac{g(u(x)-u(y))}{|x-y|^{N+sp}}\,dy\Bigg|\leq g(\|u\|_{C^{\alpha}(\R^N)})\frac{\omega_N}{(\alpha-s)p-\alpha}R^{(\alpha-s)p-\alpha}\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\int_{B_R^c(x)}|x-y|^{-N-sp}\,dy=\int_{B_R^c}|y|^{-N-sp}\,dy=\frac{\omega_N}{sp}R^{-sp}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-119: Thus, by definition of $C_{N,s,p}$, it follows that \begin{equation}\lim_{s\to 0^+}(-\Delta_p)^su(x)=g(u(x))\quadfor $x\in \R^N$.\end{equation} as claimed.
elements.sent-2411-120: Let us add some further remarks concerning $(-\Delta_p)^s$.
elements.sent-2411-121: Note that $(-\Delta_p)^s$ is also pointwisely well-defined for any $s\in(0,1)$ if $u$ is sufficiently regular, see e.g. \cite{IMS16,F23 .}
elements.sent-2411-122: To be precise, if $u\in C^{2+\alpha _c(\R^N)$ for some $\alpha>0$, then $(-\Delta_p)^su(x)$ is well-defined for all $x\in \R^N$ and $s\in(0,1)$.}
elements.sent-2411-123: The choice of the constant $C_{N,p,s}$ in our setting is rather artificial as there is no Fourier transform to justify a normalization constant as in the case $p=2$ (see e.g. \cite{NPV12 ).}
elements.sent-2411-124: With our choice, the constant agrees with the case $p=2$, the limit $s\to 0^+$ gives a kind of identity, and it holds, see for instance the discussions in \cite{TGV21,F23 , \begin{equation}\lim_{s\to1^-}(-\Delta_p)^su(x)=-\Delta_pu(x)=-\div(|\nabla u(x)|^{p-2}\nabla u(x))\quadfor all $x\in \R^N$.\end{equation} However, the choices of $C_{N,s,p}$ for $s\geq\epsilon$ for some $\epsilon>0$ is indeed not relevant to our analysis.}

elements.sent-2411-126: We begin with the introduction and study of the integral operator \begin{equation}\label{integral representation2} \begin{split} \cL_{\Delta_p} u(x) := C_{N,p}\int_{B_1(x)}\frac{|u(x)-u(y)|^{p-2}(u(x)-u(y))}{|x-y|^N}dy \quad +C_{N,p}\int_{\rz^N\setminus B_1(x)}\frac{|u(x)-u(y)|^{p-2}(u(x)-u(y))-|u(x)|^{p-2}u(x)}{|x-y|^N}dy+\rho_N\,|u(x)|^{p-2}u(x), \end{split}\end{equation} for suitable $u:\R^N\to\R$, $x\in \R^N$, and with the constants $C_{N,p}$, $\rho_N$ as in Theorem \ref{derivative .}
elements.sent-2411-127: In the first lemma, we collect some basic properties of $\cL_{\Delta_p}$ and provide an alternative integral representation of $\cL_{\Delta_p}$ which involves the function $h_{\Omega}$ defined in \eqref{h-omega function .}

elements.sent-2411-131: If $\Omega\subset \R^N$ is an open bounded set and $x\in\Omega$, then it holds \begin{equation}\cL_{\Delta_p}u(x) =C_{N,p} \int_{\Omega}\frac{g(u(x)-u(y))}{|x-y|^{N}}dy+C_{N,p}\int_{\R^N\setminus \Omega}\frac{g(u(x)-u(y))-g(u(x))}{|x-y|^N}\,dy\nonumber \qquad\qquad +\Big(\rho_N(p)+h_{\Omega}(x)\Big)g(u(x)), \label{LwithOmega}\end{equation} with $h_{\Omega}$ defined as in \eqref{h-omega function , that is, \begin{equation}h_{\Omega}(x)=C_{N,p}\int_{B_1(x)\setminus \Omega}|x-y|^{-N}\,dy-C_{N,p}\int_{\Omega\setminus B_1(x)}|x-y|^{-N}\,dy.\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-220: In this section, we give the detail of a weak formulation of problems involving the logarithmic $p$-Laplacian.

elements.sent-2411-257: In this section, we prove Theorem \ref{intro:hardy .}
elements.sent-2411-258: This proof is split into two parts.
elements.sent-2411-259: In the first one, we prove a logarithmic boundary Hardy inequality under some assumptions on the Whitney decomposition of the set.
elements.sent-2411-260: In Subsection~\ref{subs:plump , we give a~simple sufficient condition for these assumptions to hold.}
elements.sent-2411-261: As an illustration, we also prove these assumptions for the half-space, which allows us to obtain explicit ---but possibly not optimal--- constants in the logarithmic boundary Hardy inequality in this case.
elements.sent-2411-262: The last subsection is devoted to applications.
elements.subsection-2411-007: Whitney decomposition and logarithmic boundary Hardy inequality
elements.sent-2411-263: We call a~cube $Q\subset \R^N$ dyadic if its side length is equal to $2^m$ for some integer $m$ and all coordinates of its vertices are equal to an integer times $2^m$.
elements.sent-2411-264: We denote by $\cD_m$ the collection of all dyadic cubes in $\R^N$ with side length~$2^m$ and put $\cD = \bigcup_{m\in \Z \cD_m$.}
elements.sent-2411-265: Let $\cW(\Omega)$ be a~Whitney decomposition of an open set $\Omega\subset \R^N$ into cubes like in \cite{St70 .}
elements.sent-2411-267: For $m\in \Z$, let $\cW_m(\Omega) =\{ Q\in \cW(\Omega): \ell(Q) = 2^m\}$, where $\ell(Q)$ is the side length of the cube~$Q$.
elements.sent-2411-268: We assume that there exist constants $C_1$, $C_2$, $C_3$ and an integer $j_0\leq 0$ such that for each Whitney cube $Q\in \cW_k(\Omega)$ with $k<j_0$ and for each $j$ such that $k<j\leq j_0$, there exists a~Whitney cube $E(Q,j)$ with the following properties:.
elements.sent-2411-269: Additionally we assume that the following holds:.
elements.sent-2411-270: Then there is $c=c(C_1, C_2, C_3, C_4, j_0, \lambda, p)>0$ such that for every $u \in L^p(\Omega)$ \begin{equation}\label{eq:hardy} \int_\Omega |u(x)|^p \ln^+\left(\frac{1}{\delta_x}\right) \,dx \leq c \left(\ \iint_{\substack{\Omega\times \Omega |x-y|<1}}\frac{|u(x)-u(y)|^p}{|x-y|^N}\,dy\,dx + \int_\Omega |u(x)|^p\,dx \right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-271: By decreasing $j_0$ if needed, we may and do assume that $C_3 2^{j_0 < 1$.}
elements.sent-2411-272: Let $m < j_0$ and let $Q_m\in \cW_m(\Omega)$.. Then \begin{equation}\int_{Q_m} |u(x)|^p\,dx = \frac{1}{|E(Q_m,j)|} \int_{E(Q_m,j)} \int_{Q_m} |u(x)-u(y)+u(y)|^p \,dx\,dy \leq \frac{2^{p-1}\vee 1}{|E(Q_m,j)|} \int_{E(Q_m,j)} \int_{Q_m} |u(x)-u(y)|^p\,dx\,dy + \frac{(2^{p-1}\vee 1)|Q_m|}{|E(Q_m,j)|} \int_{E(Q_m,j)} |u(y)|^p\,dy \leq \frac{C_3^N (2^{p-1}\vee 1)}{C_1^N} \int_{E(Q_m,j)} \int_{Q_m} 1_{|x-y|< C_32^j}\, \frac{|u(x)-u(y)|^p}{|x-y|^N} \,dx\,dy + \frac{(2^{p-1}\vee 1) 2^{mN}}{C_1^N 2^{jN}} \int_{E(Q_m,j)} |u(y)|^p\,dy.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-273: Summing over all $j$ such that $m < j\leq j_0$ we obtain \begin{equation}(j_0 - m) \int_{Q_m} |u(x)|^p\,dx \leq \frac{C_3^N (2^{p-1}\vee 1)}{C_1^N} \int_{Q_m} \int_{\Omega\cap B_1(x)} \frac{|u(x)-u(y)|^p}{|x-y|^N} \,dy\,dx + \frac{2^{p-1}\vee 1}{C_1^N} \sum_{j=m+1}^{j_0} 2^{(m-j)N} \int_{E(Q_m,j)} |u(y)|^p\,dy.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-274: We sum over all $Q_m$ and all $m<j_0$, obtaining \begin{equation}\sum_{m<j_0} \sum_{Q_m} (j_0 - m) \int_{Q_m} |u(x)|^p\,dx \leq \frac{C_3^N (2^{p-1}\vee 1)}{C_1^N} \int_{\Omega} \int_{\Omega\cap B_1(x)} \frac{|u(x)-u(y)|^p}{|x-y|^N} \,dy\,dx + \frac{2^{p-1}\vee 1}{C_1^N} \sum_{m<j_0} \sum_{Q_m} \sum_{j=m+1}^{j_0} 2^{(m-j)N} \int_{E(Q_m,j)} |u(y)|^p\,dy =: I + S.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-275: Next, we rearrange the last sum $S$.
elements.sent-2411-276: To this end, we make the following observations.
elements.sent-2411-277: First, if $m<j_0$ and $j\leq j_0$, then by our first assumption \begin{equation}j+ \log_2 C_1 \leq \log_2 \ell(E(Q_m, j) \leq j+\log_2 C_2 \leq j_0 + \log_2 C_2,\end{equation} that is, $E(Q_m, j) \in \cW_k(\Omega)$ with $k \leq j_0 + \log_2 C_2$.

elements.subsection-2411-009: Sufficient conditions for logarithmic boundary Hardy inequality
elements.sent-2411-307: As we will show in this subsection, in Corollary~\ref{cor:hardyplump below, for inequality \eqref{eq:hardy to hold it is enough for the open set $\Omega$ to be locally plump in the sense of the following definition.}}
elements.sent-2411-308: A set $A\subset \R^N$ is locally $\kappa$-plump with $\kappa\in (0,1)$ if, for each $0<r< 1$ and each $x\in \bar{A}$, there is $z\in \bar B_r(x)$ such that $B_{\kappa r (z)\subset A$.}
elements.sent-2411-309: We also say that $A$ is locally plump, if there is some $\kappa\in(0,1)$ such that $A$ is locally $\kappa$-plump.
elements.sent-2411-310: In the usual definition of plumpness, the same is asserted for $0<r<\diam(A)$ instead of $0<r<1$ in our definition.
elements.sent-2411-311: This makes a~difference for unbounded sets only.
elements.sent-2411-312: For example, an infinite strip is locally plump, but not plump.
elements.sent-2411-313: The following lemma is the main tool in this subsection.
elements.sent-2411-314: Assume that an open set $\Omega \subset \R^N$, $\Omega\neq\R^N$ is locally $\kappa$-plump.
elements.sent-2411-316: Then for every $\omega\in \partial \Omega$, \begin{equation}\# \{Q\in \cW_n(\Omega)\,:\, Q\subset B_R(\omega)\} \le C \bigg(\frac{R}{2^n}\bigg)^\lambda\,,\end{equation} with constants $\lambda<N$ and $C$ depending only on $N$ and $\kappa$.
elements.sent-2411-317: We note that if $\Omega$ is plump, then $\partial \Omega$ is porous and hence the (upper) Assouad dimension of $\partial \Omega$ is strictly smaller than $N$, see \cite[Theorem 5.2]{MR1608518 .}
elements.sent-2411-318: The latter in turn implies condition (T1) on \cite[page 685]{DyVa .}
elements.sent-2411-319: This allows one to use \cite[Lemma 10]{DyVa , which is very similar to our Lemma~\ref{assouad .}}
elements.sent-2411-320: However, going that route would require checking what changes when one replaces plumpness by local plumpness, which we assume, and also checking if different versions of definitions are compatible.
elements.sent-2411-321: Therefore, we prefer to keep this subsection self-contained and prove Lemma~\ref{assouad directly.}
elements.sent-2411-322: To this end we need the following two simple lemmas.
elements.sent-2411-323: The proof of the first one resembles a~part of the proof of \cite[Theorem 5.2]{MR1608518 .}
elements.sent-2411-324: Then there exists a~natural number $K=K(N,\kappa)$ with the following property: for any cube $Q \in \cD_m$ with $m\leq 1$, there exists a~cube $R\in \cD_{m-K}$, $R\subset Q$ which does not contain any Whitney cube.
elements.sent-2411-325: We will show that $K$ such that $2^K > 2(5\sqrt{N /\kappa + 2)$ satisfies the property in the Lemma.}

elements.sent-2411-666: The fractional Hardy inequality\cite{Dyda, DyKi, DyVa states that for any bounded Lipschitz domain $\Omega\subset\rn$ and $0<s<1,\,p>0$ with $sp\neq 1$ there exists a constant $C=C(N,s,p,\Omega)>0$ such that \begin{equation}\label{Dyda-Hardy inq} \int_{\Omega}\frac{|u(x)|^p}{\delta_x^{sp}}dx\leq C\iint_{\Omega\times\Omega}\frac{|u(x)-u(y)|^p}{|x-y|^{N+sp}}\,dx\,dy + \int_\Omega |u(x)|^p\,dx \quad\text{ for all } u\in C_c^\infty(\Omega),\end{equation} and this inequality fails to hold in the so called critical case $sp=1$.}
elements.sent-2411-667: Recently, in \cite{AdJaRo, AdRoSa, AdRoSa2 , the authors addressed the critical case of the Hardy inequality, by inserting a logarithmic weight in the denominator on the left side of \eqref{Dyda-Hardy inq .}}
elements.sent-2411-668: We are interested in an inequality \eqref{Dyda-Hardy inq for $s=0$ and with a~logarithmic weight in the numerator of the left hand side.}
elements.sent-2411-669: However, in Section \ref{sec:hardy , we prove a much more general result (Theorem \ref{hardy ).}}
elements.sent-2411-670: We note that Theorem~\ref{intro:hardy was obtained for bounded Lipschitz set $\Omega\subset\rn$ and $p=2$, see \cite[Proposition A.1]{CW19 .}}

elements.sent-2411-685: The fractional Hardy inequality\cite{Dyda, DyKi, DyVa states that for any bounded Lipschitz domain $\Omega\subset\rn$ and $0<s<1,\,p>0$ with $sp\neq 1$ there exists a constant $C=C(N,s,p,\Omega)>0$ such that \begin{equation}\label{Dyda-Hardy inq} \int_{\Omega}\frac{|u(x)|^p}{\delta_x^{sp}}dx\leq C\iint_{\Omega\times\Omega}\frac{|u(x)-u(y)|^p}{|x-y|^{N+sp}}\,dx\,dy +C \int_\Omega |u(x)|^p\,dx \quad\text{ for all } u\in C_c^\infty(\Omega),\end{equation} and this inequality fails to hold in the so called critical case $sp=1$.}
